---
title: "Using the CLI"
description: "How to use check-datapackage on the command-line."
order: 1
---

`check-datapackage` has one command, `check`, which checks a Data
Package's metadata against the Data Package standard. The general
pattern for the CLI is:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage COMMAND [ARGS] [OPTIONS]
```

To get an overview of the available commands and flags, run the
following command, which is equivalent to using the `--help` flag:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage
```
::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}

## Output

```{python}
#| echo: false
!uv run check-datapackage
```
:::

## Checking Data Package metadata

Use the `check` command to check a Data Package's metadata:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}

### Output

The example below shows the output of running `check-datapackage check`
in a directory containing a `datapackage.json` for an example Data
Package, `flora`.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pathlib import Path

from seedcase_soil import ExampleDataPackageName, write_example_datapackage

datapackage_path = write_example_datapackage(
    Path("."),
    ExampleDataPackageName.flora,
)
!uv run check-datapackage check {datapackage_path}
```
:::

### Checking metadata in a custom location

By default, `check` looks for a `datapackage.json` file in the current
directory, but you can specify a different path using the `source`
argument:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check another/directory/datapackage.json
```

You can also point `check-datapackage` to a remote Data Package by
providing the full URL to the `datapackage.json`. For example:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle/refs/heads/main/datapackage.json
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}

### Output

```{python}
#| echo: false
!uv run check-datapackage check https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle/refs/heads/main/datapackage.json
```
:::

For Data Packages on GitHub, use either a `gh:` or `github:` prefix as a
shortcut instead of the full URL:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check gh:seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}

### Output

```{python}
#| echo: false
!uv run check-datapackage check gh:seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle
```
:::

You can also include a branch or tag:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check gh:seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle@main
```

<!-- TODO add example with error when we have a datapackge for it -->

::: callout-important
`check` raises an error when checks fail. This makes it suitable for CI
and pre-commit workflows where failed checks should stop execution.
:::

### Enabling strict checks

By default, `check` checks properties marked as "MUST" in the Data
Package standard. To additionally check "SHOULD" properties, use the
`--strict` flag:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check --strict
```

You can set `strict = true` in `.cdp.toml` as well; command-line options
override file-based configuration. See the
[configuration guide](/docs/guide/config.qmd) for details.

For more details, see the [check](/docs/guide/check.qmd) guide or run:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check --help
```

## Global options

In addition to `--help`, the top-level CLI provides:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage --install-completion
```

This installs shell completion for supported shells.

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage --version
```

This prints the installed `check-datapackage` version.
